body_txt: Zo, tell me about your mozzer. When psychology comes up in media, it will almost always use the Freudian model — even though it's archaic and many modern psychologists don't subscribe to it. Even when other schools of psychology are featured, the imagery is almost always reminiscent of psychoanalysis, usually with a couch or chaise longue to complete the scene. Freud's model (drastically oversimplified, of course) is that many psychological problems can be traced to interaction with one's parental figures or some interaction of the Id, Superego, & Ego , commonly known as the " Freudian Trio ". The best way to cure someone was the "talking cure", where the patient is made to talk about his problem until it's out in the open and he can face it directly. It is somewhat less well known for its emphasis on the psychosexual stages of child development (including oral, anal, and phallic) and the persistent belief that people can only be considered to have gone through normal childhood development if, at the ages of 3-6 they were envious of their same sex parent and intensely desirous of a sexual relationship with their opposite sex parent, and that they healthily exited this stage by learning to correctly identify with the same-sex parent. Hey, look at that, it's all that nonsense about strong mothers and weak fathers turning people gay somehow. This is why we have the comic panels up there. It wasn't only about your mother, though... it was just mostly about your mother. Freudian psychology was the predominant model, peaking in the 1920s-30s and remaining mainstream until the last quarter of the 20th century, notably when the DSM-III (1980) purged Freudian theory from its predecessor, dethroning psychoanalysis for empiricism, a shift peaking in the 1990s (known among psychologists as the "Decade of the Brain"). 1990s research on mental illness focused on neurology, electrophysiology, and brain chemistry, which can be intertwined with various mental illnesses, leading to medication becoming a major form of treatment (either by itself or in conjunction with some type of therapy). While some people choose to not take medication and instead rely on therapy alone, some therapists don't even accept clients who didn't at least try it first. This research has also led to a pop-culture simplification of mental illness as a chemical imbalance. note While even the chemical imbalance model itself is a highly controversial and largely discredited oversimplification (albeit one believed by many general practitioners), it is worth noting that mental health medications can yield positive outcomes in many individuals. Someone's personal decision to take psychiatric medications with the advice and consent of a doctor can be what it takes to get them out of a deep depression, even if it doesn't correct for some absolute electrical or chemical difference in the brain, let alone adapt the brain to a "neurotypical" baseline. In fact, some argue that depression and anxiety are the result of a normal brain's reasonable adaptation to hardship and trauma. With this considered, for many, an antidepressant is similar to an anti-inflammatory medication, like Motrin, which doesn't treat for a "Motrin deficiency" but still gets the job done. Additionally, genetics can determine how the brain handles neurotransmitters, as well as how the body metabolizes medications, so while it isn't as simple as saying you "need" a certain medication, a certain medication can still "help" you better based on genetics. Even looking purely at environmental causes of mental illness, there's the simple fact that many psychological issues can have multiple different reasons for developing outside whatever family issues someone may have. If someone has post-traumatic stress disorder because they saw a fellow soldier die in front of them, because they were raped, etc., talking about that one time their mother yelled at them as a kid isn't going to help with that. And that's without getting into the different types of therapy outside just talk therapy that can serve as a pathway to healing, much less all the different schools of thought behind them: your aforementioned soldier may find themselves in an art therapist's studio painting or making masks instead of sitting on a couch just talking, for example. The prevalence of this trope comes from Freudian psychology just being more interesting for character stories. After all, it's not particularly interesting to diagnose a character with a mental illness and treat them with appropriate drugs — but you can get a lot more mileage if you spin that illness into a Freudian Excuse like having Abusive Parents or an Oedipus Complex . Furthermore, Freudian psychoanalysis and similar humanistic therapies lend themselves well to Character Development ; they take time, but they also lead to contemplation , epiphany , and triumphant catharsis. Freudianism, even if discredited, also serves as a tact pretext for bringing up sexuality or provoking the Bourgeoisie with the Oedipus Complex and prepubescent sexuality. And just because Freudian psychology is outdated doesn't mean it's entirely useless, and psychoanalytic therapy might still be useful for some individuals. The ubiquity of this trope leads to many other psychology tropes deriving from TV-Freudianism, like the Freudian Excuse , the Freudian Couch (where the patient lies down and explains everything), and Psychological Projection . Freud Was Right is the extreme, where Freudianism is Flanderized into the idea that the subconscious id is our inner pervert , supplying an endless stream of crude sexual thoughts. And watch out for the Freudian Sex-- SLIP! We meant Slip! A subtrope of Hollywood Psych , Psychosexual Horror , and Small Reference Pools . See also Carl Jung , Freud's contemporary and one-time apprentice, who provides the most popular alternative viewpoint until, again, the late 20th century.                Oh, but how rude of me, Doctor, you asked about my mother. Well, I guess it all started when...
